message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in CURRENT code (4 hardening passes applied). Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described, the PoC reproduces, and it is genuine (not speculative/already-handled/by-design). Cite file:line.

H-023: redact_creds docstring claims the primary connection string is "always scrubbed precisely"
File: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140-143
Description: Verified: redact_creds documents 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' (line 140) via layer 1, an exact substring match text.replace(conn_str, sanitized) (line 143). psycopg/libpq error messages do not always echo the DSN verbatim: a password can be URL-decoded, re-quoted, partially rendered, or the host/port reformatted, so the exact-match replace can miss the credential and only the generic best-effort regex backstops catch it. 'always' promises a precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering.
PoC: Construct a conn_str with a percent-encoded password (e.g. 'a%2Fb') so libpq decodes it before embedding the decoded form in an error string. text.replace(conn_str, ...) does not match the decoded substring; layer 1 misses it and only the regex backstops (best-effort) apply.
Suggested fix: Change 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' to '...is scrubbed precisely when the backend echoes it verbatim; the URI-userinfo and DSN-password regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort).'

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

120	    if not host:
121	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
122	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them

127	
128	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
129	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://[REDACTED]@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
141	    """
142	    if conn_str:
143	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
144	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
145	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
146	
147	
148	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
149	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
150	
151	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
152	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
153	    """
154	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
155	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
156	
157	
158	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
159	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
160	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
161	    that can raise)."""
162	    try:
163	        pool.close()
164	    except Exception:
165	        pass
166	
167	
168	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
169	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
170	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
171	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
172	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
173	    with _init_lock:
174	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
175	    for p in pools:
176	        _safe_close(p)
177	
178	
179	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
180	    global _atexit_registered
181	    with _init_lock:
182	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
183	        if not _atexit_registered:
184	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
185	            _atexit_registered = True
186	
187	
188	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
189	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
190	    with _init_lock:
191	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
192	        if lock is None:
193	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
194	        return lock
195	
196	
197	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
198	    with _init_lock:
199	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)

content.text:
The finding is about a docstring accuracy/overclaim issue. Let me examine the exact wording and the technical claim.

The docstring at line 140 states "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely." The implementation at line 143 uses `text.replace(conn_str, ...)` which is an exact substring match.

The finding's technical claim: libpq error messages may not echo the DSN verbatim (URL-decoded passwords, reformatted host/port, etc.), so the exact-match `replace` can miss the credential. Let me verify the PoC reasoning is sound.

The PoC: a conn_str with a percent-encoded password like `a%2Fb`. libpq decodes `%2F` to `/` before connecting. If an error message embeds the decoded password (`a/b`) rather than the encoded form (`a%2Fb`), then `text.replace(conn_str, ...)` won't match because `conn_str` contains `a%2Fb`. Layer 1 misses it; only the regex backstops (layers 2-3, described as "best-effort") apply.

This is technically accurate. The word "always" combined with "precisely" overclaims: exact substring replacement only works when the backend echoes the connection string verbatim. URL-decoding, requoting, or reformatting by libpq/psycopg breaks the exact match, falling through to the regex layers which the docstring itself labels "best-effort."

However, I need to assess severity. This is a documentation accuracy issue, not a runtime defect. The actual redaction code still applies the regex backstops (lines 144-145), so credentials are still attacked by multiple layers. The risk is a misleading docstring that could lead a maintainer to over-rely on layer 1 and assume the primary credential is guaranteed scrubbed. This is a real but low-severity (doc-overclaim) issue. The finding is genuine and the PoC reproduces conceptually.

input.reasoning: Confirmed as a genuine docstring-overclaim issue in current code. At /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140 the docstring asserts "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely," but the layer-1 implementation at line 143 is a literal exact-substring replacement: text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)). Exact substring matching only succeeds when the backend echoes conn_str byte-for-byte. The PoC is sound: a DSN with a percent-encoded password (e.g. a%2Fb) is URL-decoded by libpq to a/b before use; if the error text embeds the decoded form (a/b) rather than the original encoded substring (a%2Fb), text.replace finds no match and layer 1 silently misses the credential. The same gap applies to any backend reformatting (re-quoting, host/port normalization, partial rendering). In those cases only the regex layers (_CREDS_RE at line 144, _DSN_PW_RE at line 145) catch it — and the docstring itself (lines 135-139) explicitly labels those as "best-effort." So "always ... precisely" promises a guarantee the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering; the suggested rewording is accurate. Severity is low: this is a documentation accuracy/overclaim defect, not a runtime vulnerability — the multi-layer redaction still runs unconditionally (lines 144-145 always execute), so credentials are still attacked by the regex backstops even when layer 1 misses. The concrete harm is a misleading invariant that could lead a maintainer to over-trust layer 1; no credential is guaranteed to leak because the backstops remain in force.

content.text: Confirmed H-023 as a low-severity docstring-overclaim issue. Details are in the StructuredOutput call above; the relevant code is /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140 (the "always scrubbed precisely" claim) versus the exact-substring `replace` at line 143.
